tagline: Five models. One bracket. No mercy.

round_of_64.reasoning: Multi-signal consensus: all five models agree Houston dominates this matchup.
Cross-model agreement slightly favors Texas A&M's late-season form and guard depth.
Weighted synthesis: Wisconsin's defensive metrics and experience edge out James Madison's momentum despite upset model flagging risk.
Unanimous consensus across all models: Duke's talent gap is too wide for a 13-seed.
Multi-signal synthesis flags NC State's conference tournament momentum; three of five models pick the upset.
Consensus favors Kentucky's talent, though the chaos model flags Oakland as a live upset candidate.
Slight multi-signal edge to Florida's home-state advantage and SEC schedule strength.
Cross-model consensus: Marquette's depth and defensive versatility are overwhelming for a 15-seed.
All models agree: UConn is the most complete team in the field. No upset risk.
Weighted synthesis narrowly favors Northwestern; Big Ten defensive metrics edge out FAU's post-Final Four regression.
Multi-signal consensus: SDSU's championship game experience and elite defense make them a clear favorite.
Cross-model agreement: Auburn's SEC physicality and athleticism overwhelm Yale's system.
Weighted synthesis gives BYU a slight edge; their Big 12 schedule strength outweighs Duquesne's A-10 momentum.
Consensus dominant: Illinois's size and scoring depth make this a comfortable win across all models.
Multi-signal split; weighted synthesis narrowly favors Washington State's athleticism and pace.
Cross-model consensus: Iowa State's Big 12 title credentials and defensive identity dominate.
All models agree: Purdue's Zach Edey advantage is historically dominant for a 1-vs-16 matchup.
Slight weighted edge to TCU's Big 12 experience and offensive versatility.
Multi-signal consensus: Gonzaga's perennial tournament pedigree and offensive firepower dominate.
Cross-model unanimous: Kansas's championship experience and Bill Self's tournament record are decisive.
Weighted synthesis flags Oregon's guard play and tempo as problematic for South Carolina's slower style.
Consensus dominant: Creighton's offensive system and Big East scheduling strength overwhelm Akron.
Multi-signal split: chaos and historian models flag Texas's inconsistency; weighted synthesis narrowly favors CSU's efficiency.
Cross-model consensus: Tennessee's defense and physicality are too much for a 15-seed.
All models agree: North Carolina's talent and experience make a 16-seed upset negligible.
Weighted synthesis favors Michigan State's tournament coaching pedigree and defensive identity.
Multi-signal consensus: Saint Mary's disciplined offense and WCC strength edge out Grand Canyon.
Cross-model agreement: Alabama's offensive firepower overwhelms a 13-seed.
Weighted synthesis: Clemson's ACC defensive metrics and tournament experience prevail.
Consensus dominant: Baylor's Big 12 pedigree makes this a comfortable win.
Multi-signal synthesis: Dayton's DaRon Holmes II and A-10 dominance give them the edge.
Cross-model unanimous: Arizona's Pac-12 title and offensive talent make this a blowout.

round_of_32.reasoning: Multi-signal consensus: Houston's elite defense and rebounding dominate Texas A&M.
Weighted synthesis: Duke's offensive talent and recruiting depth overwhelm Wisconsin's methodical style.
Cross-model split: upset and chaos models flag NC State's ACC tournament run; weighted synthesis narrowly picks the upset based on momentum metrics.
Multi-signal consensus: Marquette's Big East depth and guard play are too much for Florida.
All models agree: UConn's balanced attack dismantles Northwestern's defensive scheme.
Weighted synthesis: Auburn's athleticism and transition game overwhelm San Diego State's tempo control in a close call.
Cross-model consensus: Illinois's size advantage and Big Ten physicality prove decisive.
Multi-signal agreement: Iowa State's defensive identity and Big 12 depth wear down Washington State.
Consensus dominant: TCU has no answer for Zach Edey; Purdue's paint dominance is decisive.
Weighted synthesis: Kansas's championship DNA and Bill Self's adjustments narrowly edge Gonzaga's offensive system.
Multi-signal consensus: Creighton's offensive versatility and Big East experience edge Oregon.
Cross-model agreement: Tennessee's defense suffocates Colorado State's offensive limitations.
Weighted synthesis: UNC's offensive talent edges Izzo's defensive scheme in a competitive matchup.
Multi-signal consensus: Alabama's offensive firepower and tempo overwhelm Saint Mary's deliberate pace.
Cross-model agreement: Baylor's Big 12 pedigree and offensive balance edge Clemson's defense-first approach.
Weighted synthesis: Arizona's offensive talent and depth are too much for Dayton to contain.

sweet_16.reasoning: Multi-signal consensus: Houston's defensive identity and rebounding advantage neutralize Duke's offensive talent in a grind-it-out game.
Weighted synthesis: Marquette's depth and half-court execution should end NC State's run; four of five models agree.
Cross-model consensus: UConn's balanced attack and defensive versatility neutralize Auburn's athleticism.
Multi-signal split: weighted synthesis narrowly favors Illinois's size and scoring; Iowa State's defense makes this close.
Weighted synthesis: Purdue's interior dominance with Edey proves decisive despite Kansas's pedigree and coaching.
Cross-model consensus: Tennessee's suffocating defense forces Creighton into low-efficiency possessions.
Multi-signal split: weighted synthesis narrowly favors Alabama's three-point volume and transition speed over UNC's inconsistent perimeter D.
Weighted synthesis: Arizona's offensive talent and pace wear down Baylor; consensus across four models.

elite_8.reasoning: Multi-signal consensus: Houston's defensive identity and rebounding advantage prevail in a physical Elite Eight battle.
Cross-model consensus: UConn's depth and championship-caliber composure make them the clear favorite.
Weighted synthesis: Purdue's size advantage with Edey barely edges Tennessee's elite defense in a grueling matchup.
Multi-signal split: weighted synthesis narrowly favors Alabama's three-point volume and transition offense over Arizona's talent.

final_four.reasoning: Cross-model consensus: UConn's defensive versatility, scoring balance, and championship composure make them the strongest remaining team.
Weighted synthesis: Purdue's Edey advantage is historically unique; Houston's defense can't fully contain a 7'4" center.

championship.reasoning: Multi-signal consensus: UConn's complete roster, defensive switching ability, and championship experience across all five model signals converge on the Huskies as the most dominant team in the field.
